chapters.title: A Hardworking Farmer
chapters.text:
In a fertile valley where the rich, dark soil was perfect for growing crops, there lived a dedicated farmer who had spent many years carefully cultivating his land and building his reputation as one of the most successful agriculturalists in the entire region. His fields stretched as far as the eye could see, filled with golden wheat, plump vegetables, and other valuable crops that represented the fruits of his hard work and knowledge.

This farmer was particularly proud of his grain fields, where he grew the finest wheat and corn that anyone in the valley had ever seen. He had invested countless hours in preparing the soil, planting the seeds at exactly the right time, and tending to his crops with the kind of meticulous care that separated truly excellent farmers from those who were merely adequate in their agricultural endeavors.

As harvest time approached and his crops ripened to golden perfection under the warm summer sun, the farmer looked forward to reaping the rewards of another successful growing season. His grain fields promised an abundant harvest that would provide food for many families and bring him the income he needed to support his own household and continue improving his farming operation.

However, the farmer's joy at his successful crop was about to be threatened by an unexpected problem that would test both his patience and his sense of justice. Sometimes even the most careful and hardworking people find that their efforts can be undermined by circumstances beyond their control, requiring them to make difficult decisions about how to protect what they have earned through honest labor.

vocabulary.definition: people who work in farming and growing crops

comprehension.question: What made this farmer successful?
options: He was lucky with the weather
His hard work, knowledge, and meticulous care
He had the biggest farm

comprehension.question: What was the farmer most proud of?
options: His house and barn
His grain fields with fine wheat and corn
His collection of farming tools

chapters.title: Unwelcome Visitors
chapters.text:
One morning as the farmer was inspecting his nearly ready grain fields, he made a disturbing discovery that filled him with anger and frustration. A large flock of cranes had descended upon his crops during the night and were systematically eating the grain he had worked so hard to grow. These greedy birds were consuming his valuable harvest as if it were their own personal feast.

The cranes showed no respect for the farmer's property or labor. They moved through his fields in large groups, pecking at the ripened grain heads and scattering seeds carelessly on the ground. What they didn't eat, they often knocked loose from the stalks, wasting precious grain that represented months of careful cultivation and significant financial investment.

Day after day, the farmer watched helplessly as these destructive birds returned to continue their assault on his crops. They seemed to have no fear of him and appeared to consider his fields their own private feeding ground. Their daily raids were rapidly diminishing his expected harvest and threatening to turn his profitable season into a financial disaster.

The farmer realized that if he didn't take decisive action soon, these persistent thieves would destroy everything he had worked for throughout the growing season. He had tried shouting and waving his arms to scare them away, but the cranes only flew to other parts of his field and continued their destructive feeding. Clearly, more serious measures would be necessary to protect his livelihood.

vocabulary.definition: in an organized and methodical way

comprehension.question: What problem did the farmer discover?
options: His crops were not growing well
A flock of cranes was eating his grain
There was too much rain

comprehension.question: How did the cranes behave in the farmer's fields?
options: They were careful not to damage anything
They showed no respect and wasted grain carelessly
They only ate a tiny amount

chapters.title: Setting the Trap
chapters.text:
After careful consideration of his options, the farmer decided that he would have to use more drastic measures to protect his remaining crops from the persistent cranes. He spent an entire evening preparing a large net that could be spread across a section of his field, creating a trap that would capture the greedy birds when they came to feed on his grain the following morning.

The farmer worked by lamplight, carefully checking every knot and connection in his net to ensure that it would function properly when the time came to use it. He planned to hide in the nearby woods and wait for the cranes to settle into their usual feeding pattern, then spring the trap when they were too busy eating to notice the danger until it was too late to escape.

Early the next morning, just as the sun was beginning to rise above the eastern hills, the farmer positioned his trap in the area where the cranes usually began their daily feast. He scattered some extra grain around the net to make the spot even more attractive to the hungry birds, then retreated to his hiding place to wait for his unwelcome visitors to arrive.

The farmer didn't have to wait long before he heard the familiar sound of wings beating overhead and the harsh cries of the cranes as they approached his field. Right on schedule, the flock descended toward the grain-rich area where he had set his carefully prepared trap, completely unaware that their routine morning feeding was about to be dramatically interrupted by the consequences of their destructive behavior.

vocabulary.definition: extreme measures taken to solve a serious problem
moved back to a safe place

comprehension.question: What solution did the farmer decide to try?
options: Building a fence around his crops
Setting a large net trap to capture the cranes
Moving his crops to a different field

comprehension.question: How did the farmer prepare his trap?
options: He worked quickly without much care
He worked carefully by lamplight and scattered extra grain as bait
He asked his neighbors to help him

chapters.title: An Innocent Bystander
chapters.text:
As the cranes landed in the farmer's field and began their usual destructive feeding, one other bird also descended to join what appeared to be a peaceful morning meal. This was a beautiful white stork with long, elegant legs and a gentle demeanor, who had been flying overhead when she noticed the gathering of birds below. Unlike the greedy cranes, the stork was not interested in stealing the farmer's grain.

The stork had simply been searching for insects, frogs, and other small creatures that she could find among the grain stalks - the natural food that storks normally eat rather than the farmer's valuable crops. She moved gracefully through the field, pecking delicately at the ground as she hunted for the tiny animals that made up her regular diet, completely unaware of the destruction being caused by her companions.

The stork was a kind and honest bird who would never dream of stealing from hardworking farmers. She had always been respectful of human property and had never caused any damage to crops or gardens during her travels through the countryside. Her presence in the field was purely coincidental - she just happened to be in the wrong place at the wrong time.

Unfortunately for the innocent stork, her location among the grain-stealing cranes meant that when the farmer sprung his carefully prepared trap, she would be caught along with the guilty birds. Sometimes innocent individuals suffer consequences intended for wrongdoers simply because they happen to be nearby when justice is served, even though they have done nothing wrong themselves.

vocabulary.definition: people or animals who do bad or harmful things

comprehension.question: What was the stork looking for in the field?
options: The farmer's valuable grain
Insects, frogs, and small creatures - her natural food
A place to build a nest

comprehension.question: How was the stork different from the cranes?
options: She was larger and stronger
She was kind, honest, and respectful of human property
She was more colorful

chapters.title: Justice Without Mercy
chapters.text:
When the farmer saw that his trap had successfully captured the grain-stealing cranes, he felt a surge of satisfaction at finally being able to protect his hard-earned crops from further destruction. However, as he approached the struggling birds caught in his net, he discovered that his trap had also snared the innocent stork who had simply been in the wrong place at the wrong time.

The stork looked up at the farmer with frightened eyes and tried to explain her situation. "Please, kind farmer," she said in her gentle voice, "I am not like these greedy cranes who have been stealing your grain. I was only searching for insects and small creatures among your crops. I have never taken so much as a single seed from your field, and I would never dream of causing you any harm or loss."

The farmer listened to the stork's plea, but his anger over the damage to his crops had made him hard and unforgiving. All he could see was that this bird had been found among the thieves who had been destroying his livelihood, and that was enough to condemn her in his mind. He had suffered significant losses because of grain-eating birds, and he was in no mood to make careful distinctions between the guilty and the innocent.

"I don't care what your intentions were," the farmer replied coldly. "You were found among the thieves, and that is enough for me. A bird is known by the company it keeps, and if you choose to associate with grain-stealers, then you must be prepared to share their fate. I cannot and will not make exceptions, no matter how convincing your story might sound."

vocabulary.definition: to spend time with or be connected to someone

comprehension.question: How did the stork try to defend herself?
options: She tried to escape from the trap
She explained that she only ate insects and never stole grain
She blamed the cranes for everything

comprehension.question: Why didn't the farmer believe the stork?
options: He knew she was lying
His anger made him unforgiving and he judged her by the company she kept
He was too busy to listen

chapters.title: The Price of Bad Company
chapters.text:
Despite her innocence and her heartfelt pleas for mercy, the stork found herself facing the same punishment as the guilty cranes who had actually been stealing the farmer's grain. The farmer's decision to treat all the captured birds equally, regardless of their individual actions or intentions, demonstrated how sometimes innocent people can suffer consequences simply because they were in the wrong place at the wrong time.

The stork's situation illustrated an important lesson about the choices we make regarding the company we keep and the places we choose to spend our time. While she had done nothing wrong herself, her decision to land among the grain-stealing cranes had put her in a position where she would be judged not by her own actions, but by her association with those who were actually guilty of wrongdoing.

This story teaches us to be very careful about who we choose as our companions and where we decide to spend our time, because others may judge us by the company we keep rather than by our individual character or actions. Even when our own intentions are pure and our behavior is blameless, we can still face consequences if we associate with those who make poor choices or engage in harmful activities.

The farmer's harsh judgment of the innocent stork alongside the guilty cranes reminds us that the world is not always fair, and that sometimes good people suffer alongside bad people when justice is swift and unforgiving. We must choose our associations carefully, because we may be held responsible not only for our own actions, but also for the actions of those around us, whether that judgment is fair or not.

vocabulary.definition: free from fault or responsibility for wrongdoing
connections or relationships with other people

comprehension.question: What lesson does the stork's situation teach us?
options: That storks should never fly with cranes
To be careful about the company we keep because others may judge us by it
That farmers are always unfair

comprehension.question: What does this story remind us about the world?
options: That everything always works out perfectly
That the world is not always fair and good people sometimes suffer

That all birds are the same
